author: @Macken-n9l
text:
Here’s the thing I am actually not opposed to the idea of making spin-off movies about characters in major franchises. I think examples like The Penguin, Atom Eve from Invincible, and the bad batch series are all great examples of taking beloved characters and making a story of them.
But the story should always be the main focus and not a cheap plot for a quick paycheck. Which is what most media companies do these days.

author: @vinceventresca6763
text:
I loved Toy Story 1-3, especially 3. The darkness of Lotso’s Police State at Sunnyside Daycare was an incredible choice, the moments featuring Buzz in his Spanish Mode (“My What?”) may have been the funniest of all of them, and Andy’s send off was perfect. 

However, I still say that people’s minds would’ve been blown had the filmmakers faded to the end credits when Woody & Company entered the Incinerator, leaving their ultimate fate up in the air, but that’s just me.

The only thing memorable about Toy Story 4 was the scene with the toys’ attempts to drive the RV, and that was only because my wife and I had purchased an RV the previous April and appreciated the humor.

I’ve never had an inkling to return to Toy Story 4, unlike 1-3, and with the current state of filmmaking, I don’t expect Toy Story 5 to be any good.

author: @StarScreamReee
text:
Toy Story was a product of its times where people were impressed with Pixar and 3D animation and trusted Pixar to pull together a meaningful story consistently. Ideology infected the studio and the women there seeking power ousted Lasseter over hugging allegations destroying the employee cooperation. They became just another animation studio after.

Toy Story was successful as an ensemble cast of characters with woody & buzz at the forefront. This sounds like this should be a short not a full length movie but as usual the market will decide.

text: I have zero hope in Toy Story 5. No Tim Allen, no idea what it can even do make a better ending than the third one (and I quite liked the fourth one), and no John Lasseter. Say what you will about him but Toy Story was one of his pet projects and he was a big part of making them work. I shouldn’t be surprised, I don’t think Pixar have made a single film without putting at least one non-straight character since Lasseter left.

author: @mitchellalexander9162
text:
Now. We already HAD All of the Jessie development anyone could ever need with 'When She Loved Me' and her Interactions with Combat Carl in the shorts teaching her: 'Jessie Never gives up Jessie finds a way' with her claustrophobia fear.

Under normal circumstances (READ: Pre-Social Justice Age) I think most people would be mostly okay with Pixar pivoting to this and not raising too many eyebrows.

Even if you want to believe 'Okay Toy Story 4 was a bit much and kind of broke the World the Verse takes place in a bit' if you truly believe Social Justice Magically disappeared from Disney after Toy Story 4 by inflating the importance of Disney keeping a TIGHT LEASH on Pixar during Inside Out 2 (which IS a Good argument to Give this Flick a chance despite everything. Just like 'maybe' Andor Season 2) there's going to be some doubts on 5 because everyone just saw 4 as 'An Epilogue to 3' because of HOW POWERFUL and Final 3 felt. Which DOES Hurt 5's chances

But let's be honest about the BIGGEST ELEPHANT in the Room:

*These people will likely try to get the Space Rangers from Chris Hemsworth's LIGHTYEAR as Toys to shoehorn them into Canon*
